title: The neuroscience of why bilateral tapping calms the nervous system
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
The brain's rhythm problem 

 I remember sitting in my therapist's office years ago, completely numb. She asked me to cross my arms and tap my shoulders,...
htmlContent:
The brain's rhythm problem

I remember sitting in my therapist's office years ago, completely numb. She asked me to cross my arms and tap my shoulders, left then right, left then right. I felt ridiculous. A grown woman, patting herself like a child. But something shifted. A wave of warmth spread through my chest. My breathing slowed. I wasn't thinking about it. It just happened.

That was my first encounter with bilateral stimulation, and honestly, I didn't understand it for years. I just knew it worked. Here's the thing: the science behind it is both simpler and stranger than you might think. It has everything to do with how your brain processes danger, safety, and everything in between.

Your brain is constantly scanning for threats. That's its job. It's not personal. But when you've lived through complex trauma, that scanning system gets stuck in high alert. Your brain forgets how to switch gears. It stays locked in survival mode, even when you're safe. Bilateral tapping, the kind used in EMDR therapy and various somatic practices, seems to talk directly to that stuck system. It whispers, "Hey, you can relax now."

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed polyvagal theory, would say this is about your vagus nerve. That long wandering nerve connects your brain to your gut, your heart, your lungs, your face. It's the highway your nervous system uses to send signals. When you tap alternately on each side of your body, you're sending a rhythmic signal up that highway. A signal that says, "Both sides are here. Both sides are safe. No need to choose fight or flight."

Think about it. Your brain evolved to detect patterns. Rhythm matters to it. The steady beat of a drum, the rocking of a cradle, the sway of a mother's walk. These are ancient safety signals. Bilateral tapping hijacks that ancient system. It tells your brain, "This rhythm is predictable. This rhythm is safe. You can let your guard down."

Wild, right? Something so simple can reach parts of you that years of talking never touched.

What the research actually shows

Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote The Body Keeps the Score, spent decades studying how trauma lives in the body. His research shows that trauma isn't just a memory stored in your brain. It's a physical imprint. Your muscles hold it. Your gut holds it. Your nervous system holds it. And talking alone often can't reach those places.

Here's what van der Kolk found: bilateral stimulation, whether through eye movements, tapping, or alternating sounds, activates both hemispheres of your brain simultaneously. This bilateral activation seems to help your brain integrate traumatic memories. Instead of keeping them locked in the emotional, reactive parts of your brain, those memories get processed through the logical, verbal parts. They stop feeling like they're happening right now. They start feeling like what they are: things that happened in the past.

I'm not saying it's magic. It's not. It's neuroscience. But it's neuroscience that respects how your body actually works, not how we wish it worked.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Van der Kolk walks through the science in a way that doesn't feel like a textbook. He shows you why your body reacts the way it does, and what you can actually do about it. It's not a quick fix. But it's honest, and that honesty matters.

Peter Levine, another key researcher in this field, calls the rhythmic movement of bilateral tapping a way to "pendulate" between activation and settling. You tap, you feel a little more awake, a little more present. Then you pause, and you notice your body settling. Then you tap again. Back and forth. Each cycle teaches your nervous system that it can handle activation. That it can return to calm. That the pendulum doesn't have to swing only one way.

Stay with me here. This isn't about forcing yourself to relax. It's about giving your nervous system the experience of moving in and out of activation safely. Over time, that experience rewires your brain. It builds new neural pathways. Pathways that say, "I can feel this and survive. I can feel this and return to calm."

That's the real gift of bilateral tapping. Not the tapping itself, but what it teaches your body.

Why your brain resists at first

I need to be honest with you. The first few times you try bilateral tapping, it might not feel like anything. Or it might feel uncomfortable. Your brain might resist. It might throw up all kinds of distractions: boredom, skepticism, restlessness, even panic.

That's normal. That's actually a good sign.

Your nervous system has spent years, maybe decades, building its current patterns. Those patterns kept you alive. They deserve respect, not dismissal. When you introduce something new, like bilateral tapping, your brain initially sees it as a threat. "What is this? Why are we doing this? This isn't how we survive."

Think of it like this: your brain is a well-trained guard dog. It's been biting at shadows for years, keeping you safe. Now you're asking it to sit down and relax. That dog doesn't trust you yet. It's going to growl. It's going to pace. It might even snap at you.

That's okay. You keep showing up. You keep tapping. You keep offering that rhythmic signal of safety. Eventually, the dog starts to settle. It learns that you're not asking it to stop guarding. You're just asking it to rest when there's no actual danger.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link) by Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey. It shifts the question from "What's wrong with you?" to "What happened to you?" That small shift changes everything. It helps you understand why your brain resists. It's not broken. It's adapted.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link) by Peter Levine. He explains why trauma gets stuck in the body and how gentle, rhythmic movements can help release it. It's not about reliving the trauma. It's about completing the response that got interrupted.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Did your shoulders tighten? Did your breath catch? That's your nervous system responding to the idea of release. It wants to let go. It just doesn't know how yet.

How to actually do it

Here's the practical part. You can do bilateral tapping anywhere. You don't need special equipment. You don't need a therapist present. You just need your hands and a few minutes of quiet.

Cross your arms over your chest, like you're giving yourself a hug. Tap your right hand on your left shoulder, then your left hand on your right shoulder. Alternate. Find a rhythm that feels natural. Some people tap fast, some slow. There's no wrong speed.

As you tap, notice what comes up. Maybe a memory surfaces. Maybe an emotion. Maybe just boredom. Don't try to change anything. Don't analyze. Just notice. Keep tapping.

If you feel overwhelmed, slow down. Or stop. You don't have to push through. The goal isn't to feel everything at once. The goal is to let your nervous system know it's safe to feel what it's feeling, bit by bit.

You can pair this with breathing. Inhale for four taps. Exhale for four taps. Or just breathe naturally. Whatever works for you.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She walks you through simple exercises, including bilateral tapping, that help you regulate your nervous system. It's not theory. It's practice. And practice is what actually changes things.

I do this before bed most nights. Five minutes of tapping. Sometimes I fall asleep halfway through. Sometimes I end up crying. Sometimes nothing happens. All of it is fine. All of it is my nervous system doing what it needs to do.

What tapping can't do

I don't want to oversell this. Bilateral tapping is a tool, not a cure. It can calm your nervous system in the moment. It can help you process memories over time. But it can't replace therapy, medication, or community support.

If you're in the middle of a trauma response, tapping might help. It might not. Sometimes the only thing that helps is time, safety, and the presence of someone who understands. That's okay. You're not failing if tapping doesn't work for you.

Your nervous system is unique. What works for one person might not work for another. The key is to stay curious. Try things. Notice what happens. Keep what works. Let go of what doesn't.

I've been tapping for years now. Some days it feels like coming home. Other days it feels like nothing. I've learned to trust the process anyway. Because the science is clear: rhythmic, bilateral stimulation changes your brain. It might not change it overnight. But it changes it.

And that's enough. That's more than enough.

Sit with that for a moment. Your brain is capable of change. Your nervous system can learn new patterns. You are not stuck. You are not broken. You are just in process.

The tapping is just one way to remind yourself of that. But it's a good way. A gentle way. A way that honors how your body actually works, not how you wish it would.

So try it. Just for a few minutes. Cross your arms. Tap your shoulders. Breathe. See what happens. You might surprise yourself.

Healing Journey Resources

  • What Happened to You? (paid link) - A compassionate reframing of trauma by Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey.
  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) - Bessel van der Kolk's essential work on trauma and the body.
  • Anchored (paid link) - Deb Dana's practical guide to nervous system regulation.
  • Waking the Tiger (paid link) - Peter Levine's exploration of somatic trauma healing.
